key_insight: A unified planar 3D ILSA formulation, combined with linear-system splitting and Anderson acceleration, substantially reduces hydraulic-fracturing simulation time while keeping fracture-aperture error low.
engineering_relevance: These speedups make planar 3D hydraulic-fracture models more practical for design optimization, uncertainty studies, and mini-frac interpretation where many runs are needed.
method: Merged the elastohydrodynamic solver and front-tracking loops into one iterative unified ILSA scheme.
Applied matrix splitting to move the dense elasticity contribution to the right-hand side, producing a sparser linear system.
Added Anderson acceleration, and tested a predictor-corrector variant, to improve convergence across changing fracture geometries.
evidence_signal: Across five benchmark cases, the unified scheme averaged 2.5x speedup, the full method set averaged 4x, and the sandglass case reached 11x with aperture discrepancy below 5% versus the reference.
watch_item: Performance gains depend on fracture geometry and solver setup, so implementation details and convergence behavior should be checked carefully for new cases.
paper_title: Acceleration methods for the planar 3D ILSA hydraulic fracturing model
